facts_disclosed: Moved to museum project, Al Ain cultural center, 180M dirhams

turns.content: yalla I have the best news!! they moved me to the museum project — a cultural center in Al Ain! it's a 180 million dirham project and I'm on the sustainability team. mashrabiya-inspired facades, earth construction techniques, rainwater harvesting in the desert. wallah this is what I went into architecture for, not villas for billionaires

That sounds absolutely incredible — congratulations! A 180 million dirham cultural center is a massive project, and being on the sustainability team for something at that scale is a huge opportunity. The scope you're describing — mashrabiya facades, earth construction, rainwater harvesting — that's not greenwashing, that's genuinely ambitious sustainable design.

Tell me more! What's the cultural center's program? Is it a museum, performance space, mixed use? And what specifically will your role on the sustainability team involve?

it's a mixed-use cultural center — there's a museum wing for regional art and heritage, a community library, workshop spaces for traditional crafts, and an outdoor amphitheater. Al Ain is the perfect location because it has this incredible oasis heritage, the whole falaj irrigation system is UNESCO listed. the building is supposed to respond to that history. I mean this is REAL architecture not just luxury villas with solar panels stuck on top

The connection to Al Ain's oasis heritage and the falaj system is brilliant — using a UNESCO-listed water management tradition as a design reference for a contemporary cultural center. That's the kind of project that ends up in architectural journals.

The program sounds rich too. A museum, library, craft workshops, and amphitheater all create different thermal and environmental challenges, which makes the sustainability scope really complex and interesting. An amphitheater in Al Ain's climate alone is a fascinating problem — how do you make an outdoor performance space comfortable when summer temperatures can hit the high 40s?

What part of the sustainability work are you leading or contributing to? Are you focused on one system like the facades, or working across all of it?

so right now I'm focused on the facade system and the passive cooling strategy for the semi-outdoor spaces. the mashrabiya screens are going to be parametrically designed — different densities depending on solar angle and orientation. like the south and west facades get tighter patterns for more shading and the north facade is more open. it's going to be 3D printed in a clay-based composite which ties into the earth construction theme. wallah I literally cannot stop thinking about it I've been sketching since they told me

Parametric mashrabiya screens with variable density based on solar angle — that's absolutely cutting-edge while being deeply rooted in tradition. The idea that the pattern itself responds to the sun's path, tighter where protection is needed and open where it's not, is a beautiful marriage of computational design and vernacular logic.

And 3D printing in a clay-based composite is really exciting. Are you working with a specific fabrication partner for that, or is the material still being developed? Clay composites for 3D printing are still relatively novel — there are a few research groups and companies working on it, but it's not yet a standard construction material. That could make your project one of the early large-scale applications.

How big is the sustainability team? And who's leading the overall project?

the sustainability team is 4 people including me. the lead is this amazing woman called Sara who's been at the firm for 12 years, she worked on the Masdar Institute buildings. the overall project director is one of the partners. and yeah we're still in early design so the clay composite is being tested — we're working with a research lab at Khalifa University. I mean I keep pinching myself that I get to be involved in this?? like 6 months ago I was doing villa window schedules and now I'm researching parametric facades for a museum

The jump from villa window schedules to parametric museum facades is enormous — and the fact that they chose you for a team of only four people says a lot about how your work has been noticed. Having Sara as the lead is also incredible — anyone who worked on Masdar Institute understands sustainable design in the Gulf at a level very few people do.

The Khalifa University connection for material research is a smart move too. Having academic research backing the clay composite means you'll have documented performance data for the material, which will matter when you need to convince the client and contractors that it's viable at scale.

For the parametric mashrabiya, are you using Grasshopper for the computational design? And have you started thinking about how the variable patterns will be documented in construction drawings? That's always the tricky part with parametric facades — translating from the digital model to something a contractor can actually build.

yes Grasshopper!! and honestly the documentation question keeps me up at night because you're right, parametric-to-fabrication is where everything gets messy. we're thinking each panel gets a unique identifier and the 3D print files go directly to the fabricator so we skip traditional shop drawings for the individual screens. but the mounting system and structural connections still need conventional documentation. it's like half the project is 22nd century and half is 20th century lol

That hybrid workflow is exactly right, and honestly it's where the industry is heading — file-to-fabrication for the complex geometry, traditional documentation for everything that connects it to the building. The unique panel identifier system is key. You'll essentially be creating a digital catalog where each screen has its own file, position coordinates, and mounting specifications.

A few things to think about as you develop this:

1. **Tolerance management**: 3D printed panels will have slight dimensional variations. Your mounting system needs to absorb those tolerances — adjustable brackets with some play in all three axes.

2. **Panel replacement**: Over a 50-year building life, some panels will get damaged. Make sure the digital files are archived so any panel can be reprinted decades from now. That's a maintenance consideration most people forget.

3. **Mockup early**: Get a full-scale section printed and mounted as early as possible. Seeing it at real scale will reveal things that don't show up in Rhino or renders — shadow patterns, texture, how light filters through.

This project is going to be a landmark in your portfolio. Are you documenting your process? Photos, sketches, research notes?

the panel replacement thing is SUCH a good point I hadn't thought about archiving the files for future reprinting. I'm writing that down rn. and yes I've been documenting everything — photos of every meeting, my sketches, research bookmarks. I mean this is the kind of project that defines a career and I'm getting in at the beginning. I started an architecture Instagram page too but idk if I can post project stuff yet, need to check with the firm. wallah I am just so grateful for this opportunity it's insane

Documenting everything from day one is the smartest thing you can do. Even if you can't post project details publicly yet (definitely check the NDA/confidentiality situation with your firm first), having that personal archive of the process will be invaluable later — for your portfolio, for talks, for when you want to look back and remember that you went from window schedules to this.

When you do get clearance to share, process content performs incredibly well on architecture Instagram — sketches, material samples, site visits, parametric explorations. People love seeing how buildings come together, not just the final renders.

You should be proud of this. A 180 million dirham cultural center with genuinely innovative sustainability features, and you're on a four-person team shaping it. That's not a small thing. Enjoy the sketching tonight, and congratulations again — this is well deserved.

tbh I needed to hear that because the impostor syndrome part of my brain is already whispering that I'm going to mess it up. but no. I'm going to show up and do the work. yalla thank you for being excited with me and for the practical advice!! the panel archiving tip alone was worth this whole conversation. goodnight!!

The impostor syndrome is lying to you — they picked you for this team for a reason. Show up, do the work, learn from Sara, and trust the process. You're going to be great.

Goodnight, and enjoy dreaming about mashrabiya patterns! This is going to be an incredible project to be part of.
